text: Lecture	  66	  –	  Aging,	  Trauma	  and	  repair	  of	  the	  nervous	  system	   	   Key	  points	   • Increased	  longevity	  results	  in	  increased	  neurological	  disorders	   • ‘Normal’	  age-­‐related	  changes	  in	  brain	  structure	   • Neurodegenerative	  diseases	  –	  abnormal	  changes	  with	  age	   • Basic	  knowledge	  of	  Alzheimer’s	  disease	  –	  neuropathology,	  neurochemistry,	  genes	  	   • Basic	  understanding	  of	  Parkinson’s	  disease	   • Role	  of	  genetic	  versus	  environmental	  factors	   • Stroke	  –	  infarct	  and	  haemorrhage	   • Differences	  between	  CNS	  and	  PNS	  injury	   • Traumatic	  injuries	  to	  brain	  and	  spinal	  cord,	  primary	  versus	  secondary	  events	  	   • Knowledge	  of	  reactions	  to	  axon	  injury	  and	  glial	  cell	  changes	   	   Intro	   • Population	  is	  ageing	   o Loss	  of	  neurons	  and	  synaptic	  connections	   o Lose	  shape	   o Disorganization	  of	  cell	  bodies	   	   Normal	  Changes	  with	  Ageing	   • Peripheral	  processing	  changes:	  vision,	  hearing,	  balance,	  muscle	  mass,	  bone	  strength	   • Variable	  loss	  of	  grey	  matter:	  synapses	  etc.	   • Inc.	  cellular	  inclusions:	  nonliving	  material	  in	  the	  cytoplasm	  of	  a	  cell	   • Changes	  in	  level	  of	  nt	  and	  receptors	   • Altered	  blood	  vessels	   	   Strategies	  to	  avoid	  decline	   • Behavioural	  –	  Mental	  and	  physical	  exercise,	  games,	  sleep,	  etc.	   • Possibility	  to	  regenerate	  cells	   o E.g.	  granule	  cells	  in	  dentate	  gyrus	  of	  hippocampus	    Dependent	  on	  behaviours	  listed	  above	   o Stem	  cells	  divide,	  migrate	  and	  become	  mature	  neurons	   • Exercise	  α	  to	  BDNF	  protein	  which	  is	  α	  to	  new	  cell	  production	   and	  hippocampal	  volume	  (therefore	  likely	  to	  be	  an	   improvement	  in	  memory	  function)	   • Decrease	  in	  BDNF	  with	  age	   • BDNF	  is	  also	  important	  for	  plasticity,	  which	  is	  important	  for	   neural	  development	   	   Abnormal	  changes	  in	  ageing	   Alzheimers	   • macroscopic	  changes:	  Inc	  ventricle,	  hippocampus	  and	  insula,	  widened	  sulci	  and	  shrunken	  gyri	  on	  the	   cerebral	  cortex	   • microscopic	  characteristics:	  amyloid	  plaques	  and	  tangles,	  these	   often	  occur	  around	  BVs	  and	  in	  particular	  areas	  of	  the	  brain	   • Early	  onset	  due	  to:	   o mutations	  in	  the	  genes	  of	  APP,	  apoE,	  presenilins,	  tau	   o changes	  in	  Ach	  nt	  and	  receptor	  levels	   • Late	  onset	  due	  to	  idiopathic	  causes.	   	   	   	   	   Parkinson’s	   • Loss	  of	  dopamine	  connections	  from	  the	  pars	  compacta	  region	  of	  the	  substantia	  nigra	  projecting	  to	  the	   corpus	  striatum	  (you	  had	  written	  thalamus	  fyi)	   • Loss	  of	  dopamine	  connections	  is	  normal	  with	  age.	   • Parkinson’s	  has	  greater	  levels	  of	  loss	  (and	  severity	  of	  the	  disease	  α	  loss	  of	  dopamine)	   • Treat	  with	  dopamine	  drugs	  (L-­‐dopa)	  and	  cell	  transplants	   	   Huntington’s	   • Great	  loss	  of	  basal	  ganglia	  (examination	  of	  the	  brain	  shows	  atrophy	  of	  caudate	  nucleus	  mostly,	  also	  with	   some	  decrease	  in	  size	  of	  the	  putamen)	   • Characteristics:	  choreiform	  movements	  with	  progressive	  dementia	  	   • autosomal	  dominant	  inheritance	  	   	   Multiple	  Sclerosis	   • Affects	  glial	   • Affects	  oligodendrocytes	  (which	  are	  responsible	  for	  production	  of	  the	  myelin	  sheath	  of	  the	  CNS	  neurons)	   o Lose	  myelin	  and	  then	  axons	   • Autoimmune	  triggered	  by	  viral	   • More	  prevalent	  in	  equatorial	  regions,	  females	  and	  those	  of	  higher	  intelligences	   • Are	  a	  number	  of	  different	  types,	  with	  the	  most	  common	  being	  the	  relapsing	  and	  remitting	  course	   • Initial	  symptoms:	  paresthesias,	  mild	  sensory	  or	  motor	  symptoms	  	   • end	  stage:	  unsteadiness	  of	  gait,	  incontinence,	  paralysis	   	   Trauma	  and	  Vascular	  accidents	   • Affects	  nerves.	   • Haemorrhage	   o Epidural	   o Subdural	   • Stroke	  (hemorrhagic)	  	   o Intracerebral	   o Subarachnoid	   	   Regeneration	   • Minimal	  regeneration	  in	  PNS	   • Little	  or	  no	  capacity	  for	  regeneration	  in	  CNS	   o Due	  to	  inhibitory	  signals	    Inhibitory	  molecules	  on	  oligodendrocytes	  and	  central	   myelin	    Glial	  and	  meningeal	  scars	  	   o Lack	  of	  trophic	  support	  *	  what	  was	  this	  ‘*’	  for?	   	   Treating	  spinal	  cord	  injury	  	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Repair	  strategies	   • Minimize	  secondary	  injury	  due	  to	  initial	  injury.	   o Vascular,	  degenerative	  changes	  etc.	   • Harness	  any	  intrinsic	  growth	  potential	   o Protect	  compromised	  neurons	    Growth	  factors,	  pharmacological	  agents,	  gene	  therapy	   • Re-­‐establish	  pathway	  continuity	   o Cell	  replacement	    Transplantation	  of	  fetal	  cells,	  stem	  cells	   o Pathway	  reconstruction	    Bridges	  –	  cell/tissue	  transplantation,	  polymers,	  nanotechnology	   • Rehabilitation/training	   o Very	  important	   • New	  circuits,	  relays	  -­‐	  specificity	  of	  reconnection??!!	   	   Deliver	  therapeutic	  proteins	   • Systemic	  injection	  (this	  can	  be	  dangerous)	   • Local	  injection	  (problems	  with	  infection/scarring	  if	  this	  is	  need	  for	  recurrent	  injections)	  	   • Mini	  pump	   • Fibronectin	  or	  collagen	  matrix	   • Genetically	  engineered	  genes	   • Viral	  vectors	  –	  modify	  viral	  DNA	  and	  deactivate	  the	  virus	  (so	  it’s	  not	  able	  to	  replicate).	  Infect	  the	  person	   with	  the	  virus.	  Use	  the	  virus	  biomechanics	  to	  infect	  the	  host	  and	  replace	  the	  hosts	  DNA	  with	  the	  virus,	   thus	  removing	  mutated	  part	  of	  host’s	  DNA.	   	   Sources	  of	  Cells	   • Fetal	  neural	  tissue/fetal	  neurons	   • Adult	  neural	  stem	  cells	   • Bone	  marrow	   • Umbilical	  cord	  blood	   • Fetal	  neural	  stem	  cells	   • Embryonic	  stem	  cells	   • Engineered	  stem	  cell	  lines	   • Somatic	  cell	  nuclear	  transfer	  –	  ‘therapeutic’	  cloning	  Xenografts
